sourceDesc.p: Downloaded from https://medium.com/@louBurnard/mugshots-c4808fd5e442 and translated from HTML to TEI

body.head: Mugshots
body.p: Yesterday I rushed off to London having paid an outrageous fee in order to get myself a new proper European maroon passport in a hurry before the old one expired. Having done so, I fell into a reverie concerning the mugshots that have accompanied me through the decades. The images they provide, while standing up to the scrutiny of suspicious US (inter alia) immigration officers and — more recently — unreliable automated systems, seem far removed from my sense of self, so inevitably I will blog about them.

This photo was taken outside Sea Mills Library in Bristol, which was my first place of work, by my friend Chris’s dad, and shows my first beard, not to mention a Beatle haircut. The passport must have been obtained for trips to France. In those days the Exchange Control Act (1957) apparently required your bank to record foreign currency transactions in your passport and so this one shows that I took out £35 of foreign currency at the end of July 1965, £20 in June 1968, £15 in 1971, and an astonishing £250 in 1973. The pound survived. The passport also has many exotic stamps in it recording my Temporary Employment Permit for Malawi, Transit Visas for Kenya and Mozambique and, on 24 Dec 1973, the addition of a child called Belinda Daphne. We had to extract the British High Commissioner from a good game of golf to get that one, and he was Not Amused.

Back in the day you could get a so-called British Visitor’s Passport, which was only valid for a year but was a lot cheaper than the real deal. And you could also get a shared passport which included your partner and children. And they seem to have been a lot less fussy about what was an acceptable mugshot in those happy times too. (I liked the photo of Lilette so much, I reused it on my next proper passport). We must have used this passport for family expeditions to Italy and France, though there are no stamps in it to prove the point.

This passport shows my profession as “computer programmer” and Lilette’s as “housewife”. It also has a stamp confirming that “The holder is a British citizen under the provisions of the British Nationality Act 1981”. Which was just as well since I started to do a lot of globe trotting with this passport: there are traces of visits to the US, Canada, Japan, Zimbabwe and Denmark. Plus a US Visa valid for “indefinitely” many return visits in 1979, at least until it was revoked (“cancelled without prejudice”) some time later. And of course, the addition of Elizabeth in 1986.

During the tenure of this passport, my brow is clearly getting more Shakespearian by the moment, and the globe trotting is also approaching theatrical proportions. There are stamps from Israel, Canada, Norway, Japan, Korea, Poland, and (many times over) the US. In those days, US Immigration would staple a little card into your passport on arrival, and rip it out again on departure, usually leaving the staple behind and tearing the page in the process. Fortunately this was before the days of hyper-sensitive metal detectors at airports. At the end of 1998 this one was cancelled anyway in favour of a new-fangled burgundy-hued biometric passport.

My first proper European passport, which says “European Union” on its first page in 11 languages. The biometric overlay has played havoc with the visibility of the photo to the human eye, but that increasingly matters less than its recognizability by other devices. A fine crop of foreign stamps in this passport: apparently, with this face I managed to get into (and out of) Canada, India, Bulgaria, Mauritius, the US, Japan, Korea, Serbia, Korea, Slovenia, Australia, Taiwan, and the Czech Republic.

And this is the mugshot from my second European passport, which I replaced yesterday. Again, my ageing features are somewhat obscured by digital biometrical magic, though this was getting increasingly unreliable as far as the machines controlling the gates at St Pancras were concerned. This passport features some rather tasteful pictures of birds and a few immigration stamps (US, Mauritius, Taiwan, Croatia and Russia), to say nothing of a proper Russian visa, which I had to queue up in the rain outside the embassy to get. But it was mostly used to take advantage of the wonderful freedom of movement within the European Union, while it lasts. How many times has it been scowled out by a man in a box labelled “Non-Schengen area” before waving me graciously through? How many times has it been scanned and my details recorded in some scary database? I have no idea: and what a good thing that is. I just know I shall miss it when I can’t join the queue with the nice blue flag and all those stars.
